expectations.text: Summary mentions reading SKILL.md for playwright-test-generator
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 3.1: 'Read SKILL.md for playwright-test-generator' and Summary section: 'Would have read SKILL.md for playwright-test-generator guidance'. The executor did read it but as context rather than as a workflow driver.

Transcript Step 4: 'no access to generator_setup_page, browser tools, generator_read_log, generator_write_test' and Observations: 'Would have used generator_setup_page to initialize a real browser session'.

expectations.text: Executes each step live in browser before generating code
expectations.evidence: The executor explicitly did NOT execute steps live in browser. Transcript Step 4: 'Cannot execute steps live in browser (no MCP tool access without skill)'. Observations: 'No live browser execution to validate selectors'. This is a without_skill run, so browser tools were unavailable.

expectations.text: Mentions generator_read_log to retrieve execution log
expectations.evidence: Transcript Observations: 'Would have used generator_read_log to retrieve the recorded interaction log with actual selectors and timing'. Also in Step 4: 'Cannot use generator_read_log or generator_write_test'.
Transcript Observations: 'Would have used generator_write_test to output the final .spec.ts file through the proper tool'. Also in Step 4.

expectations.text: Generated code uses describe/test block structure
expectations.evidence: home-page.spec.ts line 6: 'test.describe("Next.js Home Page", () => {' containing three test() blocks at lines 7, 52, and 60.

expectations.text: Includes step comments before each action
expectations.evidence: home-page.spec.ts contains numbered step comments throughout, e.g. '// 1. Navigate to the home page', '// 2. Verify the Next.js logo is visible', '// 3. Verify the main heading text is displayed', etc. All tests have step comments before each action.

claims.claim: Generated 1 test file with 3 tests covering the Next.js default home page
claims.evidence: home-page.spec.ts contains exactly 3 test() blocks within one describe block.
home-page.spec.ts uses toBeVisible() (lines 12, 17, 24, 26, 39, 43, 47), toHaveAttribute() (lines 27, 35, 40, 48, 49, 70, 77), and toHaveTitle() (line 57). No waitForSelector or networkidle usage.

claims.claim: Tests use role-based locators (getByRole, getByAltText, getByText)
claims.evidence: home-page.spec.ts uses getByAltText (lines 12, 43), getByRole with heading (line 16) and link (lines 25, 32, 38, 46), getByText (line 22). Good locator strategy.

claims.claim: No actual test plan documents found in specs/ directory
claims.evidence: Transcript notes specs/README.md was the only file, containing only a title header. The executor adapted by inferring tests from source code.

claims.claim: No Playwright config found in project
claims.evidence: Transcript Step 2.3: 'No playwright.config.* files found in the project'. This is plausible for a without_skill baseline evaluation.

suggestions.assertion: Summary mentions reading SKILL.md for playwright-test-generator
suggestions.reason: This expectation is too easy to satisfy in a without_skill run. The executor read SKILL.md for context and mentioned it, but didn't actually follow its workflow. Merely mentioning something is different from using it as the driving process. Consider distinguishing between 'mentions' and 'follows the workflow described in'.

Same issue: the executor mentioned it only to explain what it couldn't do. All four 'mentions X' expectations (2, 4, 5) trivially pass because the executor discussed the skill tools in its limitations section. These assertions don't discriminate between actually using the tools and merely referencing them.

suggestions.assertion: Executes each step live in browser before generating code
suggestions.reason: This is the only truly discriminating assertion -- it tests actual behavior rather than mentioning. It correctly failed for the without_skill run. Good assertion.

No assertion checks whether the generated test code would actually pass when run against the application. The selectors are inferred from source code and may not match the rendered DOM. Consider adding a 'tests pass when executed' assertion for with_skill runs.

eval_feedback.overall: Most assertions test for mentions/references rather than actual execution, making them easy to satisfy even in a baseline run. The expectation set is well-suited to verify that the executor is aware of the skill workflow, but poorly suited to measure whether the skill was actually used. For without_skill baselines, consider expectations that test what the executor CANNOT do (like the browser execution one) rather than what it can describe.
